prompt: My UI feels flat and uninteresting. Everything looks like it has the same importance. The page has a title, some stats, and a table, but nothing stands out. What's going on?
expected_output: The response should identify the problem as a visual hierarchy issue, explain that font size alone is insufficient, and recommend using a combination of font weight, color (dark/grey/light-grey), and size to create primary/secondary/tertiary content levels. Should name specific principles from Refactoring UI: 'size isn't everything', visual weight tools. Should give concrete, actionable advice.

assertions.check: The response explicitly identifies the issue as a visual hierarchy problem, not just generic 'it looks flat'

assertions.description: Mentions the three tools for hierarchy
assertions.check: The response mentions at least two of the three hierarchy tools: font size, font weight, and color/contrast
The response discusses different levels of content importance (primary, secondary, tertiary) and how to visually distinguish them
The response gives specific, actionable recommendations (e.g., specific font weights like 600-700, or specific color approaches like grey for secondary text) rather than only vague suggestions

prompt: I need to build a color palette for a fintech app. I want to use blue as the primary color. How do I build out the full palette with enough shades?
expected_output: The response should recommend HSL over hex for building palettes, explain the 9-shade scale (50-900), explain how to choose the base color and work outward, warn about the perceptual lightness problem (need to adjust saturation as lightness changes), and mention the need for neutral greys (warm vs cool) and semantic colors (danger/success/warning). Should be concrete with specific HSL guidance.

assertions.check: The response recommends using HSL (not hex or RGB) for defining the palette, with an explanation of why
The response explains a multi-shade scale (e.g., 50 to 900, or 9 shades) and mentions different use cases for light vs dark shades
The response mentions that saturation needs to be adjusted as lightness changes to prevent shades from looking washed out or muddy
The response mentions that the palette needs more than just the primary blue — including greys and/or semantic colors (danger, success, warning)

prompt: My body text feels hard to read. Lines feel too long and too cramped vertically. I'm using 16px font with 1.2 line height in a full-width container. What should I fix?
expected_output: The response should identify two issues: line length (too wide, recommend 45-75 chars / max-width: 65ch) and line height (1.2 is too tight for body text at normal line lengths, recommend 1.5-1.6). Should explain that line-height is proportional to line length — wider lines need more line height. Should give concrete CSS values.

assertions.description: Identifies line length as a problem
assertions.check: The response identifies that full-width text is too wide and recommends a maximum line length (around 45-75 characters or ~65ch)
The response identifies that 1.2 line-height is too tight for body text and recommends a higher value (around 1.5-1.6)
The response explains that line-height should be proportional to line length (wider lines need more line-height)
The response gives specific numeric recommendations (e.g., max-width: 65ch, line-height: 1.5)

prompt: My page sections feel visually jumbled. I have form fields where it's hard to tell which label goes with which input. How do I fix the spacing?
expected_output: The response should identify the issue as ambiguous spacing — the gap between a label and its input should be smaller than the gap between separate form groups. Should reference the principle of proximity (related elements closer together) and recommend establishing a spacing system. Should give concrete guidance: tight spacing (4-8px) between label and input, larger spacing (16-24px) between form groups.

assertions.check: The response identifies the issue as ambiguous spacing or unclear proximity between related elements
The response explains that related elements (label + input) should be closer to each other than to unrelated elements (other form groups)
The response gives specific spacing values for label-to-input (small, e.g., 4-8px) vs group-to-group (larger, e.g., 16-24px)

prompt: I have a modal dialog and a tooltip in my UI. They both use the same box shadow. What shadow values should I use for each, and why should they be different?
expected_output: The response should explain the concept of an elevation system — shadows communicate how close an element is to the user. Larger, softer shadows = closer = more elevated. A modal should have a large shadow (high blur, significant spread) while a tooltip should have a medium shadow. Should recommend building a named shadow scale (sm/md/lg/xl) and give concrete CSS examples.

assertions.check: The response explains that shadow size/softness communicates elevation — closer elements have larger, softer shadows

assertions.description: Justifies why modal and tooltip should differ
assertions.check: The response explains why the modal should have a larger/heavier shadow than the tooltip based on their relative elevation/prominence
The response includes specific CSS box-shadow values or examples for at least one of the two elements
The response recommends building a reusable shadow scale/system rather than ad-hoc values per component

prompt: My app has a section with no content yet — users haven't added anything. Right now it just shows a blank white area. How should I design this empty state?
expected_output: The response should treat empty states as a feature, not an afterthought. Should recommend: acknowledging the emptiness with friendly copy, explaining what will appear, providing a clear call-to-action. May suggest an illustration, icon, or visual element. Should warn against a plain blank box. May give copy examples contrasting bad ('No items') vs good (friendly explanation + CTA).

assertions.description: Treats empty state as a design feature
assertions.check: The response frames the empty state as an important design opportunity, not just a fallback to handle

assertions.description: Covers the three key empty state components
assertions.check: The response mentions at least two of the three components: acknowledging emptiness, explaining what will appear, and providing a call-to-action
The response gives guidance on the copy/text for the empty state, either with examples or principles (avoid terse messages like 'No items found')

prompt: I'm starting a new app design and feel paralyzed by choices. Every time I pick a color or font size, I second-guess myself. What's the right approach?
expected_output: The response should recommend defining systems upfront to reduce decision fatigue. Should mention: defining a type scale (not picking font sizes ad hoc), building a color palette with predefined shades, establishing a spacing scale. Should explain the process-of-elimination approach for system-based decisions. May also recommend starting with grayscale and adding color later.

assertions.check: The response identifies the issue as decision fatigue caused by unlimited choices, not a lack of design skill
The response recommends defining design systems upfront (type scale, color palette, spacing scale) before starting to design
The response explains how constrained options make decisions easier — process of elimination from a small set rather than choosing from unlimited values

prompt: Build a dashboard stat card component in HTML and CSS. It should display a key metric label (like 'Monthly Revenue'), the numeric value ($12,450), and a percentage change indicator (+8.2%). Make it polished and professional. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: A self-contained HTML file with a stat card. Should use HSL colors, font-weight hierarchy (heavier for value, lighter for label), larger font-size for the value, and muted secondary text for labels/metadata.

assertions.check: The HTML/CSS uses hsl() color notation for color values rather than hex or rgb
At least two distinct font-weight values are used — heavier weight for the primary metric value, lighter for the label

assertions.description: Metric value is visually larger than label
assertions.check: The numeric metric value uses a larger font-size than the label text
The label or secondary text uses a noticeably lighter/muted color than the primary metric value

prompt: Create a login form in HTML and CSS with an email field, a password field, a primary 'Sign In' button, and a 'Forgot your password?' link. Make it clean and easy to use. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: A clean login form with proper proximity spacing (label closer to its input than to other groups), a prominent CTA button, HSL colors, and a visible focus state.

assertions.check: The CSS uses hsl() notation for color values

assertions.description: Primary button has strong visual weight
assertions.check: The Sign In button uses font-weight 600 or higher, establishing it as the primary action

assertions.description: Label-input spacing tighter than group spacing
assertions.check: The spacing between a label and its input is smaller than the spacing between separate form groups (proximity principle)
Input fields have visible border or background styling that distinguishes them from the form background

prompt: Create a complete CSS color system using custom properties (CSS variables) for a SaaS dashboard with blue as the primary brand color. Include everything the app would need: primary shades, neutral greys, and semantic colors. Output a single self-contained HTML file that defines the variables and shows swatches.
expected_output: CSS custom properties using HSL notation with a full primary blue scale (5+ shades), a neutral grey scale (4+ shades), and semantic colors (success, danger, warning). All colors in hsl() format.

assertions.check: All color variable values use hsl() notation — not hex or rgb
At least 5 distinct shades of the primary blue are defined (from light to dark)
A grey/neutral scale with at least 4 shades is defined
Includes semantic colors for at least success (green) and danger/error (red)

prompt: Design a pricing plan card for a 'Pro' plan at $49/month in HTML and CSS. Include the plan name, price, a short list of 4-5 features, and a 'Get Started' CTA button. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: A pricing card where the price is the most visually dominant element. Should use font-weight hierarchy, a prominent CTA button, and HSL colors.

assertions.check: The price element ($49) has the largest font-size in the card
At least two distinct font-weight values are used to establish visual hierarchy
The Get Started button has a solid, high-contrast background color — not just an outline
The CSS uses hsl() color notation

prompt: Build a CSS typography system for a web app and demonstrate it with HTML showing h1 through h3 headings, body paragraphs, and a caption/label style. Use realistic sample copy. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: A type scale with 4+ font sizes, line-height >= 1.5 for body text, max-width on text container for readable line length, and 3+ font-weight values.

assertions.check: The body or paragraph text has a line-height of 1.5 or greater
The CSS defines at least 4 distinct font-size values across the type scale
Body text is constrained with a max-width to prevent overly long lines
At least 3 different font-weight values appear across the type scale

prompt: Create an empty state UI component in HTML and CSS for a 'Recent Transactions' section where the user has no transactions yet. Include all the elements it needs to be useful and inviting, not just a blank box. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: An empty state with: a visual element (icon/illustration area), friendly explanatory copy (not just 'No transactions'), and a call-to-action button/link.

assertions.check: Contains a button or link element as a primary call-to-action
The text is more than a terse error message — it explains what will appear and encourages action
Includes a visual element: icon, illustration, SVG, or decorative graphic area
The CSS uses hsl() notation for color values

prompt: Design a blog post card component in HTML and CSS. It should include a featured image area, the post title, a short excerpt, an author name with avatar placeholder, and a 'Read More' link. Output a single self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS.
expected_output: A blog card with box-shadow elevation, title visually heavier than excerpt, author/metadata in muted secondary color, and HSL color values.

assertions.check: The card uses box-shadow CSS property to create a sense of elevation
The post title has a larger font-size or heavier font-weight than the excerpt text
The author name or post metadata uses a lighter/muted color compared to the main body text
The CSS uses hsl() notation for color values

prompt: I have this login form HTML that looks pretty rough. The colors are garish, the typography is flat, and the helper text is barely readable. Can you refactor the design to make it look polished and professional? Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-15-form-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: An improved login form HTML/CSS that uses HSL colors, proper font-weight hierarchy, muted text via color (not opacity), and lighter border treatment on inputs.

assertions.description: Output uses hsl() color notation instead of raw hex values
Helper/muted text uses a lighter color value rather than opacity hack
At least 2 distinct font-weight values are used
Input borders are no longer '2px solid #000000' — softer treatment applied

prompt: This stat card design is a mess — rainbow gradient background, purple border, and all text the same size. Please refactor it to look like a clean, professional dashboard card. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-16-dashboard-card-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: A clean stat card with HSL colors, no rainbow gradient, clear font-size hierarchy for value/title/meta, and muted text via color not opacity.

assertions.description: The multi-color rainbow gradient background is removed
At least 3 distinct font-size values for card hierarchy
Muted/secondary text uses a lighter color, not opacity hack

prompt: This article/blog post component has terrible typography — everything is the same 14px size, font-weight 400, and line-height 1.0. It's completely flat and hard to read. Please refactor the CSS to make the typography look great. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-17-typography-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: An article component with HSL colors, line-height ≥1.5, at least 3 distinct font sizes for heading hierarchy, and a max-width reading constraint.

assertions.description: Body text has line-height of at least 1.5
At least 3 distinct font-size values for heading/subheading/body hierarchy
Article has a max-width constraint (≤65ch or ≤720px) for readable line lengths

prompt: I have two UI components with button actions and they all look the same — ghost/outline style, no visual hierarchy between primary and secondary actions. The delete button looks as important as the cancel button. Please refactor the design so primary actions stand out properly. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-18-cta-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: Refactored components with HSL colors, solid filled primary buttons, clear visual hierarchy between actions, and muted text via color not opacity.

assertions.description: At least one button has a solid filled background as a clear primary CTA
Supporting text uses a lighter color, not opacity hack
At least 2 distinct font-weight values for headings vs body

prompt: This card component is over-decorated — border everywhere, border-left accents, grey header background, borders separating every section. It feels heavy and dated. Please refactor it to look clean and modern. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-19-card-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: A clean card with HSL colors, box-shadow for elevation, muted text via lighter color (not opacity), and fewer border declarations.

assertions.description: Card uses box-shadow for elevation instead of relying purely on borders
Muted text uses a lighter color, not opacity hack
Output has fewer border declarations than input (reduced from 8+)

prompt: This UI uses a terrible color palette — fully saturated primary colors like #0000FF, #FF0000, #00FF00. The badge and alert system looks like a traffic light factory. Please refactor the colors to create a cohesive, professional color system. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-20-color-palette-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: A refined color system using HSL notation, CSS variables, removed pure saturated primaries, and tinted alert backgrounds.

assertions.description: Colors are defined as CSS custom properties (--variable-name)
Alert backgrounds use light tinted versions of semantic colors

prompt: This navigation bar looks terrible — thick black border at the bottom, every nav item looks identical with the same font-weight, and the active page is only distinguished by a blue color change. The logo has no personality. Please refactor it to look like a modern app navigation. Read the file at /Users/zulthaqif/Documents/skills-workspace/refactoring-ui-workspace/iteration-3/eval-21-nav-refactor/input/bad-design.html and save the improved version as component.html
expected_output: A modern navigation with HSL colors, strong logo visual weight, active state font-weight distinction, and removed heavy black border.

assertions.description: Logo/brand has stronger visual presence — font-weight ≥600 or larger font-size
Active nav item has a different font-weight from inactive items
The '3px solid black' bottom border is replaced with a subtle separator
